topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The official notice sat on my desk like a dead weight, crisp white paper mocking the stale air of the student union. "Closure effective June 1st." I didn't even bother reading the fine print. I just stared at the embossed seal, my jaw tight. I slammed the folder shut, the sound echoing too loudly in the empty room. People were going to cry, they’d start begging for extensions, but I wasn't going to sit there and crumble. I grabbed my backpack, the weight of textbooks suddenly feeling like armor. I needed to get out, to prove that this crumbling edifice couldn't dictate my next move. The feeling in my chest wasn't fear; it was a hard, vibrating energy, a refusal to be boxed in.

[story 2]

"So, that's it?" I asked the advisor, my voice flat, deliberately devoid of inflection. I leaned back in the uncomfortable plastic chair, crossing my arms so tightly my knuckles turned white. The air in the office felt thick, suffocating, but I met his gaze steadily. "You think this changes anything? This place is just bricks and mortar, Professor. It’s not the curriculum, it’s not the knowledge we absorbed here that matters. We took what we needed. We're not going to let this administrative hiccup derail everything." I stood up abruptly, pushing past the cluttered filing cabinets. My steps were quick, purposeful, each movement a declaration that I was steering myself away from the impending disaster, not being swept along by it.

[story 3]

The campus grounds looked strangely quiet, the usual hubbub of late-afternoon students replaced by an eerie stillness. I walked past the main administration building, my boots crunching on scattered autumn leaves. A knot tightened in my stomach, but instead of pulling back, I walked faster. I found a forgotten, overgrown patch of lawn near the old library—a small, defiant island in the decay. I sat down heavily on the damp grass, pulling my worn sketchbook out. I didn't look at the notice anymore; I started drawing, furiously sketching the shadows, the way the light fractured through the thinning canopy. If they wanted to shut the doors, fine. I’d make my own space right here, in the margins of what was being erased.
